one_line_takeaway: OEU reframes machine unlearning in quantized neural networks as 'maximize predictive entropy on the forget set, then orthogonally project that gradient off the retain gradient,' and its own ablations show both pieces are needed: removing entropy-guided unlearning leaves the model under-forgetting (FA gap widens, MIA gap worsens), while removing orthogonal projection lets the forgetting gradient damage retain accuracy.
key_finding: On quantized ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 at 10% forget ratio, OEU attains FA 93.58% (gap 0.20% from Retrain's 93.38%) and RA 99.86% (gap 0.14%), for an Average Gap of 0.58%, versus Q-MUL's AG of 1.30% (Q-MUL's FA gap was 3.30%, RA gap 0.33%) -- OEU's AG is roughly 55% lower than Q-MUL's at this setting. At 30% and 50% forget ratios on CIFAR-10, OEU's AG is 1.31% and 1.37% respectively, versus Q-MUL's 1.68% and 1.91%. On CIFAR-100, OEU's AG is 3.08% (10%), 1.98% (30%, ~49% lower than Q-MUL's 3.90%), and 3.67% (50%, versus Q-MUL's 7.06% and SalUn's 7.33%). The CIFAR-100/30% ablation (Table 2) shows OEU's full AG of 1.98% degrades to 3.53% when GOP is removed (RA gap rises from 1.58% to 2.85%, FA gap from 1.26% to 4.51%) and to 3.98% when EGU is removed (FA jumps to 75.10% vs Retrain's 67.67%, i.e., incomplete forgetting, and the MIA gap worsens from 2.11% to 7.85%, indicating weaker privacy protection); replacing layer-wise GOP with a single global projection (GOP_global) is worse still, AG 5.40%. These ablation deltas are the paper's own evidence that both EGU and the layer-wise orthogonal projection contribute independently to the result, not just the combination.
core_contribution: The paper proposes OEU (Orthogonal Entropy Unlearning), a machine-unlearning framework specifically for quantized neural networks (QNNs) that replaces label-manipulation forgetting with entropy maximization and replaces scalar gradient reweighting with an explicit orthogonal-projection step. It has two components, both original to this paper: (1) Entropy-Guided Unlearning (EGU), which minimizes a loss equal to the negative entropy of the model's predictive distribution on the forget set (Eq. 7), driving predictions toward the uniform distribution (max entropy = log K) instead of toward a specific wrong label, and (2) Gradient Orthogonal Projection (GOP), which projects the forgetting gradient g_f onto the orthogonal complement of the retain gradient g_r (Eq. 8, with a layer-wise normalized variant in Eq. 9-10 and an interpolation strength alpha), so that forgetting updates provably do not increase the retain loss under a first-order approximation (Theorem 4.3), while still guaranteeing a strict decrease in forget loss when g_f is not collinear with g_r (Theorem 4.4). The paper also proves (Theorems 4.1/4.2) that label-manipulation unlearning methods converge to a biased one-hot distribution over a wrong class (KL divergence from uniform = log K) whereas EGU converges to the true uniform distribution (KL divergence = 0), formalizing the paper's claim that prior QNN unlearning conflates 'forgetting' with 'confidently misremembering.'
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the classic sense; this is a privacy-compliance / data-removal setting. The setup: a quantized model M0 is trained on dataset D; a subset Df must be erased on request (GDPR 'right to be forgotten') without full retraining; the retained subset Dr's accuracy must be preserved. Privacy leakage from residual influence of 'forgotten' data is quantified via Membership Inference Attack (MIA) success rate (Shokri et al., 2017) as one of the paper's five evaluation metrics, but the paper does not construct a novel attacker or attack methodology -- MIA is used purely as a downstream privacy-leakage probe on the unlearned model, following prior unlearning-evaluation protocols (Jia et al. 2023; Fan et al. 2024; Tong et al. 2025).
limitations: Notable caveats visible from the paper's own reported numbers: (1) OEU's gains over Q-MUL are consistent but often modest in absolute AG terms (e.g., 0.58% vs 1.30%, 1.31% vs 1.68%) except on CIFAR-100 at higher forget ratios where the gap is larger; (2) GOP's theoretical guarantee (Theorem 4.3, that retain loss is unaffected) holds only 'under first-order approximation,' i.e., for a single small gradient step, not as an exact guarantee for the full multi-epoch unlearning trajectory used in practice; (3) the paper introduces a hyperparameter alpha to control orthogonalization strength but the reviewed sections do not report sensitivity/ablation results for alpha; (4) evaluation is restricted to two backbones (ResNet-18, MobileNetV2) and low-bit quantization configurations (4-bit and 2-bit) which the authors adopted 'consistent with Q-MUL' for comparability, so generalization to other architectures/bit-widths is not directly evidenced in the reviewed sections; (5) the IU (Influence Unlearning) baseline collapses catastrophically at higher forget ratios (e.g., FA/RA/TA all drop to ~16% or lower at 50% forgetting on CIFAR-10, and to ~1-4% on CIFAR-100), which the paper reports as a baseline weakness rather than a limitation of OEU, but it is worth flagging that this makes IU a very weak comparator at high forget ratios.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10, 10% forget: OEU FA 93.58% (gap 0.20%), RA 99.86% (gap 0.14%), AG 0.58% vs Q-MUL AG 1.30% (FA gap 3.30%, RA gap 0.33%); Retrain FA 93.38%, RA 100.0%
CIFAR-10, 30% forget: OEU AG 1.31% vs Q-MUL AG 1.68%
CIFAR-10, 50% forget: OEU AG 1.37% vs Q-MUL AG 1.91%
CIFAR-100, 10% forget: OEU AG 3.08% vs Q-MUL AG 3.11%
CIFAR-100, 30% forget: OEU FA 66.41% (gap 1.26% from Retrain 67.67%), OEU AG 1.98% vs Q-MUL AG 3.90% (~49% lower)
CIFAR-100, 50% forget: OEU AG 3.67% vs Q-MUL AG 7.06% and SalUn AG 7.33%

Ablation (CIFAR-100, ResNet-18, 30% forget, Table 2): full OEU AG 1.98%; w/o GOP AG 3.53% (RA gap 1.58%->2.85%, FA gap 1.26%->4.51%); w/o EGU AG 3.98% (FA 75.10% vs Retrain 67.67%, MIA gap 2.11%->7.85%); GOP_global AG 5.40%
